nodes.name: Native American History
nodes.description: Major Native American nations, leaders, and events in U.S. history

items.prompt: This alliance of northeastern woodlands nations — Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, Seneca, and Tuscarora — is often cited as one of the world's oldest participatory democracies. Its Great Law of Peace may have influenced the U.S. Constitution. What is it called?
items.explanation:
Alliance of six nations in present-day New York and surrounding areas; self-name is Haudenosaunee ('People of the Longhouse')
Original five nations: Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, Seneca — the Tuscarora joined in 1722, making it the Six Nations
Governed by the Great Law of Peace (Gayanesshagowa) with 50 sachems (peace chiefs) — possibly established in the 15th century
Congress formally acknowledged in 1988 that the Confederacy influenced the formation of the U.S. Constitution (H.Con.Res.331)
Split during the American Revolution: Mohawk, Cayuga, Onondaga, and Seneca allied with Britain; Oneida and Tuscarora with the colonies
The Onondaga Nation near Syracuse, NY still functions as the Confederacy's capital and issues its own passports

items.prompt: This Southeastern nation had its own written syllabary, a bilingual newspaper, and a constitutional government modeled on the U.S. system — yet was forcibly removed from Georgia to Indian Territory in 1838. What nation is this?
items.explanation:
Originally centered in the southeastern U.S. (Georgia, Tennessee, North Carolina); one of the 'Five Civilized Tribes'
Sequoyah created the Cherokee syllabary (~85 characters) in 1821 — the only known instance of one person independently creating a complete writing system
Published the Cherokee Phoenix (1828), the first Native American newspaper, in both Cherokee and English
Adopted a written constitution in 1827 modeled on the U.S. Constitution, with legislative, executive, and judicial branches
Forcibly removed on the Trail of Tears (1838–1839); ~4,000 of 16,000 Cherokees died during the march
Today the Cherokee Nation in Oklahoma is the largest federally recognized tribe with ~450,000 citizens
items.cardFront: Southeastern nation with its own syllabary

items.prompt: This Southwestern nation has the largest reservation in the United States, spanning parts of Arizona, New Mexico, and Utah. During World War II, members of this nation served as 'code talkers,' creating an unbreakable military code. What nation is this?
items.explanation:
Self-name is Dine ('The People'); primarily an Athabaskan-speaking people of the American Southwest
Largest Native American reservation in the U.S. at ~27,000 square miles (roughly the size of West Virginia) spanning Arizona, New Mexico, and Utah
Navajo Code Talkers in WWII used their language to create an unbreakable code in the Pacific Theater — ~400 served; the code was never broken by the Japanese
Second-largest federally recognized tribe with ~400,000 enrolled members
Known for distinctive art forms: turquoise and silver jewelry, sandpainting, and Navajo rugs (woven by women as a sacred art)
The Long Walk (1864): ~8,000 Navajos were forced to march 300 miles to Bosque Redondo internment camp in New Mexico; allowed to return in 1868

items.prompt: This Great Plains nation, the western branch of a broader alliance of related peoples, defeated Custer at the Battle of the Little Bighorn in 1876 and suffered the Wounded Knee Massacre in 1890. Their leaders included Sitting Bull, Crazy Horse, and Red Cloud. What nation is this?
items.explanation:
Western branch of the Sioux (also called Teton Sioux); a nomadic buffalo-hunting nation of the Great Plains
Part of the broader Sioux alliance with the Dakota (eastern) and Nakota (central) — 'Sioux' derives from an Ojibwe word meaning 'little snakes'
Defeated Custer at the Battle of the Little Bighorn (June 25, 1876) — the most famous Native American military victory
Suffered the Wounded Knee Massacre (December 29, 1890): U.S. 7th Cavalry killed ~250–300 Lakota, including women and children
Leaders include Sitting Bull (spiritual leader), Crazy Horse (war leader), and Red Cloud (who led Red Cloud's War, 1866–1868, the only war a Native nation clearly won against the U.S.)
The Black Hills of South Dakota are sacred to the Lakota; the Supreme Court ruled in 1980 that the land was taken illegally, but the Lakota have refused the $1+ billion settlement, demanding the land's return
items.cardFront: Great Plains nation; Little Bighorn and Wounded Knee

items.prompt: This Hunkpapa holy man and leader unified the Native coalition that defeated Custer at the Little Bighorn in 1876. He later toured with Buffalo Bill's Wild West show before being killed by Indian agency police in 1890. Who was he?
items.explanation:
Hunkpapa Lakota holy man and war leader (c. 1831–1890)
Led the coalition of Lakota, Cheyenne, and Arapaho forces that defeated Custer at Little Bighorn (June 25, 1876)
Fled to Canada after Little Bighorn; returned and surrendered in 1881 after his followers faced starvation
Toured with Buffalo Bill Cody's Wild West show in 1885 — reportedly gave away much of his earnings to homeless children he encountered
Killed by Indian agency police on the Standing Rock Reservation (December 15, 1890) during an attempt to arrest him over fears he would join the Ghost Dance movement
His death occurred just two weeks before the Wounded Knee Massacre, which effectively ended armed Native resistance on the Plains
items.cardFront: Hunkpapa Lakota holy man at Little Bighorn

items.prompt: This Lemhi Shoshone woman joined the Lewis and Clark Expedition in 1805 as an interpreter and guide while carrying her infant son. She is one of the most memorialized women in American history, appearing on the U.S. dollar coin. Who was she?
items.explanation:
Lemhi Shoshone woman (c. 1788–1812 or possibly 1884) who accompanied the Lewis and Clark Expedition (1805–1806)
Kidnapped by Hidatsa raiders as a child and later married French-Canadian fur trader Toussaint Charbonneau
Carried her infant son Jean Baptiste ('Pomp') throughout the expedition — Clark later helped raise and educate the boy
Her presence signaled peaceful intentions to Native groups the expedition encountered — a war party would not travel with a woman and baby
Appears on the U.S. Sacagawea dollar coin (introduced 2000); more statues in her honor than any other American woman
Spelling varies by region: 'Sacagawea' (Shoshone derivation), 'Sacajawea' (common variant), 'Sakakawea' (Hidatsa derivation, used in North Dakota)
items.cardFront: Shoshone guide on the Lewis and Clark Expedition

items.prompt: This Chiricahua Apache leader led guerrilla raids against Mexican and American forces for decades in the Southwest. He was the last major Native leader to formally surrender to the U.S. government, in 1886. Who was he?
items.explanation:
Chiricahua Apache leader and medicine man (1829–1909); birth name Goyahkla ('One Who Yawns')
Led guerrilla resistance against Mexican and U.S. forces across the American Southwest for over 30 years
Surrendered to General Nelson Miles in Skeleton Canyon, Arizona on September 4, 1886 — the last significant Native American leader to surrender
With only 36 followers, he evaded 5,000 U.S. soldiers and 3,000 Mexican soldiers — one of the most remarkable guerrilla campaigns in military history
Spent the last 23 years of his life as a prisoner of war at Fort Sill, Oklahoma; became a celebrity and appeared at the 1904 World's Fair
U.S. paratroopers adopted 'Geronimo!' as a battle cry during WWII jumps — the code word for the Osama bin Laden raid in 2011 was also 'Geronimo'

items.prompt: This Shawnee leader attempted to unite Native nations across the eastern United States into a confederacy to resist American expansion. He was killed fighting alongside the British at the Battle of the Thames in 1813. Who was he?
items.explanation:
Shawnee leader and orator (c. 1768–1813) who sought to create a pan-Indian confederacy to resist U.S. westward expansion
Traveled thousands of miles from the Great Lakes to the Gulf Coast recruiting nations to his alliance
His brother Tenskwatawa ('The Open Door'), known as The Prophet, provided spiritual leadership for the movement; their base was Prophetstown on the Tippecanoe River in Indiana
William Henry Harrison destroyed Prophetstown at the Battle of Tippecanoe (November 7, 1811) while Tecumseh was away recruiting
Allied with the British in the War of 1812; killed at the Battle of the Thames in Ontario, Canada (October 5, 1813)
Harrison used 'Tippecanoe and Tyler Too' as his 1840 presidential campaign slogan, capitalizing on his victory over Tecumseh's forces
items.cardFront: Shawnee leader who sought pan-Indian confederacy

items.prompt: This Oglala Lakota war leader helped defeat Custer at the Little Bighorn and won the Battle of the Rosebud eight days earlier. He surrendered in 1877 and was killed at Fort Robinson. A massive sculpture in his likeness has been under construction in the Black Hills since 1948. Who was he?
items.explanation:
Oglala Lakota war leader (c. 1840–1877); Lakota name Tashunke Witko
Led warriors to victory at the Battle of the Rosebud (June 17, 1876) and the Battle of the Little Bighorn eight days later
Known for leading from the front — his bravery in battle was legendary among the Lakota
Surrendered at Fort Robinson, Nebraska in May 1877; stabbed to death by a soldier on September 5, 1877, under disputed circumstances
No confirmed photograph exists — he reportedly refused to be photographed, saying 'Why would you wish to shorten my life by taking from me my shadow?'
The Crazy Horse Memorial, a mountain carving in the Black Hills, has been under construction since 1948 — if completed, it will be the largest sculpture in the world (563 feet high)
items.cardFront: Oglala Lakota war leader at Little Bighorn

items.prompt: In 1838–1839, the U.S. Army forcibly marched ~16,000 members of a Southeastern nation over 1,000 miles from Georgia to Indian Territory (Oklahoma). About 4,000 died on the journey. What is this forced migration called?
items.explanation:
Forced removal of the Cherokee from their homelands in Georgia, Tennessee, and the Carolinas to Indian Territory (Oklahoma), 1838–1839
Authorized by the Indian Removal Act (signed by Andrew Jackson, May 28, 1830) and enforced by General Winfield Scott with 7,000 troops
Approximately 4,000 of 16,000 Cherokee died from exposure, disease, and starvation during the march
The Cherokee had won their case at the Supreme Court (Worcester v. Georgia, 1832) — Jackson reportedly said 'John Marshall has made his decision; now let him enforce it'
The term also applies broadly to the removal of the Choctaw, Seminole, Creek, and Chickasaw ('Five Civilized Tribes') during the 1830s
The route is now a National Historic Trail covering approximately 2,200 miles across nine states

items.prompt: On December 29, 1890, U.S. 7th Cavalry troops massacred an estimated 250–300 Lakota men, women, and children at a creek in South Dakota. It is often considered the end of the Indian Wars. What is this event called?
items.explanation:
Occurred December 29, 1890, at Wounded Knee Creek on the Pine Ridge Reservation in South Dakota
U.S. 7th Cavalry killed an estimated 250–300 Miniconjou Lakota and Hunkpapa Lakota, including many women and children
The troops were disarming the Lakota when a shot was fired — soldiers then opened fire with rifles and Hotchkiss guns
Twenty soldiers received the Medal of Honor for the massacre — a source of ongoing controversy and calls for revocation
Often considered the last major armed conflict of the American Indian Wars
The site was occupied by American Indian Movement (AIM) activists for 71 days in 1973 in a standoff with federal agents, drawing renewed attention to Native American rights

items.prompt: On June 25, 1876, a combined force of Lakota, Northern Cheyenne, and Arapaho warriors annihilated five companies of the U.S. 7th Cavalry led by Lieutenant Colonel George Armstrong Custer in Montana Territory. What is this battle called?
items.explanation:
Fought June 25–26, 1876, along the Little Bighorn River in Montana Territory (present-day Crow Reservation)
Custer and approximately 210 men of the 7th Cavalry were killed — the most famous military defeat in the Indian Wars
Native forces numbered approximately 2,000 warriors led by Sitting Bull (spiritual leader) and Crazy Horse (war leader)
Custer divided his regiment into three battalions and attacked without reconnaissance — a fatal tactical error
The victory was short-lived: the U.S. military launched massive reprisals, and most of the Lakota and Cheyenne surrendered within a year
The Lakota name for the battle is 'Battle of the Greasy Grass'; the battlefield is now Little Bighorn Battlefield National Monument

items.prompt: This Powhatan woman reportedly intervened to save Captain John Smith's life at Jamestown in 1607. She married tobacco planter John Rolfe, traveled to England, and died there in 1617 at about age 21. Who was she?
items.explanation:
Powhatan woman (c. 1596–1617); birth name Amonute, also known as Matoaka — 'Pocahontas' was a childhood nickname meaning 'playful one'
Daughter of Wahunsenacah (Chief Powhatan), paramount chief of a confederation of ~30 Algonquian-speaking tribes in the Virginia Tidewater
The story of her saving John Smith's life (1607) was first published by Smith himself in 1616 — many historians question its accuracy
Captured by English colonists in 1613; during captivity she converted to Christianity, was baptized as 'Rebecca,' and married John Rolfe (1614)
Traveled to England in 1616 as a diplomatic celebrity; died at Gravesend, England in March 1617 at about age 21 (cause unknown)
Her marriage to John Rolfe created a period of peace (the 'Peace of Pocahontas') between the Powhatan and Jamestown colonists (1614–1622)

items.prompt: This Cherokee silversmith created a complete writing system of approximately 85 characters for his people's language around 1821 — the only known instance of a single person independently inventing a functional script. Who was he?
items.explanation:
Cherokee silversmith and scholar (c. 1770–1843) who created the Cherokee syllabary
Developed a writing system of 85–86 characters over approximately 12 years, completing it around 1821
The only known person in history to independently create a complete and functional writing system without being literate in any existing script
After adoption by the Cherokee Nation in 1825, Cherokee literacy rates reportedly surpassed those of surrounding white settlers within a few years
The Cherokee Phoenix, the first Native American newspaper (1828), was printed using his syllabary alongside English
The giant sequoia trees (Sequoiadendron giganteum) and Sequoia National Park in California are named in his honor
items.cardFront: Cherokee inventor of a writing system

items.prompt: This Chiricahua Apache chief led a decade of resistance against the U.S. Army in the Southwest (1861–1872), fighting from mountain strongholds in present-day Arizona. He negotiated a peace agreement that created a reservation on Apache homeland. Who was he?
items.explanation:
Chiricahua Apache chief (c. 1805–1874) who led armed resistance in present-day southeastern Arizona
The Bascom Affair (1861) — a wrongful accusation of kidnapping by Lt. George Bascom — triggered Cochise's 11-year war against the U.S. Army
Fought alongside his father-in-law Mangas Coloradas until Mangas was killed under a flag of truce by U.S. soldiers (1863)
Used the Dragoon Mountains of Arizona as his stronghold — his guerrilla tactics made the region nearly impassable for settlers
Negotiated peace with General Oliver O. Howard in 1872, securing a reservation on Chiricahua homeland in southeastern Arizona
Died of natural causes in 1874; his burial place in the Dragoon Mountains has never been found — Geronimo later continued the Apache resistance
